response.description_long:
For finance teams managing payouts across multiple payment processors, the monthly close is a spreadsheet marathon. Exports from Stripe, Adyen, PayPal, and bank feeds must be manually matched against invoices and ledger entries using VLOOKUPs and manual checking — a process that routinely consumes the first week of every month. The real pain is not the volume of transactions, but the exceptions: a payout that nets out three refunds and a currency conversion, a chargeback that lands two months after the sale, a processor fee schedule that changed mid-month. Each exception costs an hour in a spreadsheet, and the same ones resurface every close.

Ledgerline automates payment reconciliation by connecting directly to payment processors and bank accounts through read-only integrations. Its matching engine starts at the payout level: for every deposit that hits the bank, it reconstructs the set of charges, refunds, chargebacks, and fees inside it using processor reports and invoice data. Multi-currency payouts are decomposed with the processor's own conversion rate, so FX differences post to the correct account. Partial payments, over-payments, and invoices settled across two payouts are treated as first-class cases. Every match carries a confidence score; high-confidence matches post automatically under team-approved rules, while everything else lands in a review queue with evidence attached. Accountants can accept a suggested resolution, and Ledgerline offers to turn that decision into a rule so the same pattern resolves itself next month. The engine posts journal entries to NetSuite, QuickBooks, or Xero through each system's official API — summarized or at transaction level, per the team's configuration.

The review queue is where the team's judgment gets captured. Each exception shows the payout line, candidate transactions, the mismatch (amount, date, currency, or missing counterpart), and a suggested resolution. Customers typically see their exception queue shrink by more than half within three closes as rules accumulate. A close dashboard tracks each processor's status: transactions ingested, matched percentage, exceptions open, entries posted. When every lane is green, reconciliation is done. Every number posted is traceable to its sources — clicking any journal line reveals the payout, underlying transactions, the matching rule, and the approver — giving auditors a read-only seat and controllers close-cycle analytics to show the CFO that the close is actually getting faster.

Ledgerline pricing is usage-based: a flat platform fee plus a per-transaction rate that declines with volume, metered on transactions reconciled (not ingested), with no implementation fee for standard integrations. The platform fee covers unlimited users, all ledger integrations, and support. Standard integrations include Stripe, Adyen, PayPal, bank feeds, NetSuite, QuickBooks, and Xero. Volume discounts apply automatically as transaction count grows; annual billing earns a discount. Finance teams typically connect their first processor in an afternoon and complete their first assisted close within one cycle. Ledgerline supports multi-entity reconciliation, backfills up to 24 months of processor history, and lets customers keep their chart of accounts with per-processor and per-entity account mapping.

Ledgerline positions itself as a narrow and deep solution to a specific problem: model the payout properly, capture the team's judgment as rules, and make the evidence inspectable. The company was founded in 2021 in New York by Elena Marsh, a former controller, and Daniel Okoye, a payments engineer who built payout systems inside a processor. They realized both sides of the reconciliation problem — the spreadsheet and the payout file — were generated by software, and only the matching in between was still manual. Over 400 companies use Ledgerline, reporting month-end close times cut by 70 percent and exception queues that started in the hundreds settling into the dozens. The company is SOC 2 Type II certified, never holds funds, and has 32 people across New York and Toronto.
